content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
July 19,
1995
John Pimentel
Deputy Secretary
Business,
Transportation
and Housing Agency
801
K Street,
Suite 1918
Sacramento,
CA 95814-3520
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-224
Dear Mr.
Pimentel:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
potential filing obligations of members of the Commission on
Transportation Investment under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act •(the 11
Act”) •1
QUESTION
Are members of the Commission on Transportation Investment
(“CTI”)
public officials under the Act and required to file
statements of economic interests?
CONCLUSION
Because the CTI is not a decisionmaking commission,
the
Business,
Transportation and Housing Agency is not required to add
the CTI to its conflict of interest code and the members are not
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
et .
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
S of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Pimentel,
John
NO. A-95-224
Page 2
required to file statements of economic interests.
FACTS
On June 22,
1995,
Dean R.
Dunphy, Secretary of the Business,
Transportation and Housing Agency,
issued a Secretary’s Order
creating the CTI.
The order authorized the CTI to hold public
hearings to receive input regarding’ the following issues:
(1)
determining spending priorities and efficiencies forCalifornia’s
entire transportation program;
(2)
evaluating the State
Transportation Improvement Plan and its ability to accurately
align programmed delivery targets with actual revenue;
(3)
estimating state revenues for 2-10 years;
(4) proposing policies
that encourage the efficient movement of goods, people and
information;
(5)
evaluating the long-term cost implications of
operating current investments in rail and rubber-tire transit
systems;
(6)
enumerating the potential impacts of Congressional
reauthorization of the Intermodal Surface Transportation
Efficiency Act,
the National Passenger Railroad Act and other
related federal laws;
(7) pointing out challenges and
opportunities for, successful implementation of the “Information
Superhighway” and its impact on transportation infrastructure;
(8)
evaluating Caltrans’ progresson implementation of the
recommendations made in the February 1994 SRI Management Audit.
It is the mission of the CTI to report to the Secretary of
the Business, Transportation and Housing Agency with a range of
policy options by November 15,
1995,
which in regard to the
state’s transportation system promote cost effective management,
realistic planning and programming based on solid economic
assumptions and clear priority setting in infrastructure
investment and maintenance.
The CTI will also report revenue
neutral policy options for long-term transportation issues such as
improving fuel efficiency,
Implementation.of alternative fuels and
scheduled expiration of local sales tax measures:
ANALYSIS
Government Code Section 87300 requires every state or local
government agency to adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest
code.
Conflict of interest codes are required to identify
designated employees,
and assign to those designated positions
appropriate categories of financial disclosure.
Each conflict of
interest code must specifically enumerate the positions within the
agency which involve making or participating in making
governmental decisions which may foreseeably have a material
financial effect on any economic interest.
(Section 87302.)
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to
“public officials,” including “designated employees.”
(Section
87100.)
The term “designated employee” is defined in Section
82019 to specifically exclude members of board arid commissions
which serve a solely advisory function.
Regulation 18700 (a) (1)


Pimente!,
John
NO. A-95-224
Page3
provides guidelines in determining whether a board or commission
is “solely advisory” or hasdecisionmaking authority.
It
provides:
•“•Public official at any level of state or local
government” means a member, officer, employeeor
consultant of a state or local government agency...
“Member” shall include but not be limited to,
salaried or unsalaried members of boards or commissions
with decisionmaking authority.
A board or commission
possesses decisionmaking authority whenever:
(A)
It may make a final governmental
decision;
(B)
It may compel a governmental decision; or
it may prevent a governmental decision by
reason of an exclusive power to initiate the
decision or by reason of a veto which may not
be overridden; or
(C)
It makes substantive recommendations
which are,
and over an extended period of time
have been, regularly approved without
significant amendment or modification by
another public official or governmental
agency.
A board or commission which does not possess decisionmaking
authority pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18700 (a) (1)
is
solely advisory in nature.
Accordingly, its members are not
public officials and need not be included in a conflict of
interest code.
Since the CIT is a solely advisory body, it is not necessary
to include it in your agency’s conflict of interest code.
2
2
Under Regulation 18700 (a) (1) Cc),
members of the CIT may
become public officials subject to the Act’s requirements if their
recommendations over an extended period of time are routinely
approved without significant amendment or modification by another
public official or government agency.
It is our understanding
that the CIT sunsets in November,
1995.
If it continues beyond
that date you should contact this office for additional advice.


Pimentel,
John
NO.
A-95-224
Page
4
If you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916)
322-5662.
•
Sincerely;
Steven G. Churchwell
G neral Couns
By:
Warne P.
Imberi
Pdftitical Reform Consultant
